claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 19 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand a bit about your situation.

**First, let me ask about the child or children you're claiming for:**

1. **How old is your child?** (If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can assess each one)
   - Under 16 years old
   - 16-19 years old  
   - 20 or over

The age of the child is important because:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age and their name so we can proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'm wondering if I can claim Child Benefit. I have a daughter, Alex, who's 19 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me help you determine if you can claim Child Benefit for Alex, your 19-year-old daughter.

Since Alex is 19 years old, she falls into the 16-19 age category. For children in this age group, eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now, I need to know about Alex's current situation regarding education or training:**

**Is Alex currently:**
1. **In approved education or training?** (This includes full-time non-advanced education like A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3, or approved training programs)

2. **Recently left education or training?** (Within the last 20 weeks)

3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

Please let me know which situation applies to Alex so I can continue the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is still in education - she's doing her A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is doing A-levels at sixth form college, she is in approved education. Let me check the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! A-levels are approved (non-advanced) education. Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education and work situation:

**1. Is Alex's A-level course full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study - which is typical for sixth form A-levels)

**2. Is this education funded by an employer or connected to an agreed future job with an employer?**

**3. Does Alex have any paid work?** If yes, how many hours per week does she work?

Please let me know these details so I can continue the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's a full-time A-level course at the college. No, it's not funded by any employer - it's just regular sixth form. And no, Alex doesn't have any paid work at the moment - she's focusing on her studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Since Alex isn't working, that's fine. Now I need to check a couple more things:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in her own right?** Specifically:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Tax credits

These are benefits that would be claimed by Alex herself, not through you. Is Alex receiving any of these benefits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't receiving any benefits in her own right. She's still living at home with me and is just a student.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! One more quick question about Alex's education:

**Is Alex doing an apprenticeship, or is it just regular A-levels?**

(Most apprenticeships would disqualify someone from Child Benefit, but since you mentioned it's A-levels at sixth form college, I just need to confirm she's not in an apprenticeship program.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, it's just regular A-levels - not an apprenticeship. She's doing traditional sixth form studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check your situation as the claimant. 

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?** You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working/studying/have sufficient resources
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? (Most people simply normally live in the UK)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - I live here in England with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to confirm:

**Are you responsible for Alex?** You can meet this requirement if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (which you've already mentioned you do)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying for food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you, it sounds like you live together. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else

If there is someone else claiming, are you both in agreement about transferring the claim to you?

What's the situation with any other potential claimants?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of these situations applies to Alex?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (which seems to be the case)
2. Currently in hospital
3. In local authority care
4. Being fostered

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Is that correct, or is there any special living arrangement I should know about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex is just living normally at home with me. No special arrangements or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Just one final question:

**Is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

(This would be unusual if Alex is living at home with you, but I need to confirm the council isn't providing financial support for Alex's upkeep.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex - I'm supporting her myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Excellent News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements** to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

### Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex qualifies as your child** - She's 19 and in full-time approved education (A-levels at sixth form college)  
✅ **Education requirements met** - A-levels are non-advanced, full-time education (12+ hours/week)  
✅ **Work status** - Alex isn't working 24+ hours per week  
✅ **Benefits status** - Alex isn't receiving benefits in her own right  
✅ **Not an apprenticeship** - She's doing traditional A-levels  
✅ **Your residency** - You normally live in the UK  
✅ **Your responsibility** - You live with Alex and support her  
✅ **No competing claim** - Nobody else is claiming for Alex  
✅ **Living arrangements** - Alex lives normally at home with you  
✅ **Council support** - The council isn't paying for Alex's upkeep

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online** at www.gov.uk/child-benefit or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Payments** will be made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important - High Income Child Benefit Charge:

⚠️ Be aware: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge through a tax return. You can still claim, but you might need to pay some or all of it back through tax.

**More information:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about how to apply or anything else?

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and in full-time approved (non-advanced) education, specifically A-levels at sixth form college. The course is at least 12 hours per week of supervised study and is not employer-funded. Alex is not working (less than 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in her own right (ESA or Universal Credit), and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK, lives with Alex and is responsible for her, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex lives normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 19-year-old daughter Alex who is studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 19 years old and in full-time approved (non-advanced) education, specifically A-levels at sixth form college. The course is at least 12 hours per week of supervised study and is not employer-funded. Alex is not working (less than 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in her own right (ESA or Universal Credit), and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK, lives with Alex and is responsible for her, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex lives normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 19-year-old daughter Alex who is studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college.
